narrative: As a white male applicant with a lower-middle family income background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles of past students. The model estimated a 60.2% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to succeed right away. The primary reason for this decision traces back to your academic performance in school, particularly your placement in the lowest decile for grades in Year 3, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure. This low Year 3 ranking, well below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack position, signaled to the model a potential struggle with the rigorous demands ahead, like those on the bar exam. Your Year 1 grades, also in the lowest decile compared to averages, reinforced this concern as the second-most influential factor, suggesting an early pattern that weighed heavily against you. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, though below the average of 3.21, actually worked in your favor as the third-most important feature, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing and softening the overall risk. Other elements, like your LSAT score of 34—lower than the typical 36.7—and your plan to attend full-time, which is common for most students, played supporting roles but weren't the main drivers. In comparison to typical applicants who pass the bar, your early and later school deciles were notably weaker, placing you outside the stronger academic profiles the model favors. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven call to minimize the school's risk of investing in someone predicted to need multiple bar attempts. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters highlighting your resilience and growth since those early low deciles. Reapplying after addressing these core academic signals could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at this or other programs. You're clearly motivated to pursue law—channel that into targeted improvements, and the numbers can change in your favor.
